coredata.dc:title: Self-cascade catalytic system constructed using carbon dots and Au nanoparticles co-assembled on MIL-53(Fe)-NH2 as a three-in-one fluorescent nanozyme for multimodal detection of glucose and maltose in food

coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Junxue
Wang, Yufei
He, Mingqin
Chen, Xiaohong
Zhou, Chenyu
Li, Jiyang
coredata.dc:description: Determination of glucose and maltose is crucial for food production and human health. Herein, a novel Au/CD@MIL-53(Fe)-NH2 self-cascade nanozyme was constructed via host–guest assembly with “three-in-one” features, including blue fluorescence, H2O2 production as an oxidase mimic, and •OH generation as a peroxidase mimic. Theoretical and experimental results proved that the incorporation of carbon dots renders the composite with high peroxidase-like activity and extremely high affinity for H2O2 (K m : 0.011 mM), 336 times higher than that of the natural enzyme. The self-cascade catalytic process could oxidize colorless 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) to blue ox-TMB, which further quenched the fluorescence and produced a photothermal effect. Consequently, colorimetric/fluorescence/photothermal-based multimodal detection of glucose and maltose was first achieved, obtaining low detection limits of 12/7.5/1.1 and 18/11.3/1.4 μM, respectively. This study offers a promising strategy for designing efficient nanozyme-based cascade systems that help enhance food quality determination.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nanozyme
Self-cascade reaction
Metal–organic framework
Multimodal analysis
Nanoparticles

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Multimodal detection of glucose and maltose
Immobilization of CDs/Au NPs in MIL-53(Fe)-NH2

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM and HRTEM images of (A, B) CDs@MIL-2 and (C, D) Au/CDs@MIL-3 (inset: lattice spacing of Au NPs). Dark-field TEM (E, F) and elemental mapping (G) of Au/CDs@MIL-3. (H) XRD patterns, (I) XPS spectra of Fe 2p, and (J) FL emission spectra of CDs and MOF-based materials.

UV–vis absorption spectra of (A) reaction solutions containing TMB + CDs@MIL-2 or H2O2 and TMB + H2O2 + different series of materials. (B) CDs@MIL-2 for oxidation of various substrates and (C) TMB + H2O2 + Au/CDs@MIL. The kinetic curves of (D, G) MIL-53(Fe)-NH2, (E, H) CDs@MIL-2, and (F, I) Au/CDs@MIL-3 with H2O2 and TMB as substrates, respectively. The insets are the corresponding double reciprocal plots. Gibbs free energy changes of (J) MIL-53(Fe)-NH2 and (K) CDs@MIL-2 on the corresponding reaction paths.

UV–vis absorption spectra of the self-cascade oxidation system with (A, B) different catalysts and substrates, (C) CAT, (E) glucose and maltose, and (F) reagents based on the specific colorimetric assay. (D) EPR spectra of Au/CDs@MIL-3 with glucose and maltose. (G) Illustration of the self-cascade sequential oxidation process.

(A) UV–vis absorption, (D) FL, and (G) temperature changes of Au/CDs@MIL-3 with TMB in the presence of different concentrations of glucose/maltose. (B) and (C) Change in absorbance at 652 nm, (E) and (F) (FF0)/F0, (H) and (I) change in temperature versus glucose and maltose concentrations. Selectivity of (J) colorimetric, (K) FL, and (L) photothermal detection of glucose and maltose.
Illustration of the preparation and application of Au/CDs@MIL-53(Fe)-NH2 using an oxidase-peroxidase cascade for multimode detection of glucose and maltose.
Recovery of glucose from spiked milk samples by the Au/CD@MIL-3 nanozyme.

head.ce:title: Self-cascade catalytic system constructed using carbon dots and Au nanoparticles co-assembled on MIL-53(Fe)-NH2 as a three-in-one fluorescent nanozyme for multimodal detection of glucose and maltose in food

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Analytical Chemistry, College of Chemistry, Jilin University, Changchun 130012, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Analytical Chemistry, College of Chemistry, Jilin University, Changchun 130012, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Determination of glucose and maltose is crucial for food production and human health. Herein, a novel Au/CD@MIL-53(Fe)-NH2 self-cascade nanozyme was constructed via host–guest assembly with “three-in-one” features, including blue fluorescence, H2O2 production as an oxidase mimic, and •OH generation as a peroxidase mimic. Theoretical and experimental results proved that the incorporation of carbon dots renders the composite with high peroxidase-like activity and extremely high affinity for H2O2 (K m : 0.011 mM), 336 times higher than that of the natural enzyme. The self-cascade catalytic process could oxidize colorless 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) to blue ox-TMB, which further quenched the fluorescence and produced a photothermal effect. Consequently, colorimetric/fluorescence/photothermal-based multimodal detection of glucose and maltose was first achieved, obtaining low detection limits of 12/7.5/1.1 and 18/11.3/1.4 μM, respectively. This study offers a promising strategy for designing efficient nanozyme-based cascade systems that help enhance food quality determination.

Two functional guests of oxidase-like Au nanoparticles (Au NPs) and luminescent carbon dots (CDs) were introduced into peroxidase-like MIL-53(Fe)-NH2 to construct an efficient nanozyme for self-cascade sequential oxidation system, and a sensitive colorimetric/fluorescent/photothermal biosensor was first developed for detection of glucose and maltose.
Unlabelled Image

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Glucose and maltose are vital carbohydrates and sources of energy that are widely distributed in various foods. Their content in food considerably affects the taste of food, and their excessive intake can result in dangerous diseases, such as diabetes, uremia, and heart disease (Tan et al., 2024). Therefore, the food industry must optimize production processes, improve product quality, and safeguard human health to conduct a quantitative analysis of glucose and maltose in food. Multienzyme cascade catalysis as a series of typical biochemical reactions has several advantages such as good selectivity, high catalytic efficiency, and signal amplification (Cheng et al., 2023). Inspired by this, cascade catalysis based on nanozymes has garnered enormous attention for food quality detection (Li, Bao, et al., 2024; Li, Xie, et al., 2024). Even today, majority of the cascade-based biosensors for glucose and maltose sensing are composed of artificial and natural enzymes and constructing a self-cascade nanozyme system to achieve efficient detection of glucose and maltose in food remains challenging (Jiang et al., 2024).

To this end, a feasible method would be to integrate various enzyme mimics into a platform to prepare hybrid nanomaterials by multicomponent synergy (Li, Bao, et al., 2024; Li, Xie, et al., 2024; Ye et al., 2023). However, the enzyme-mimetic activity and affinity for intermediate H2O2 in the reported cascade systems are insufficient as these only realize single colorimetric sensing based on their intrinsic activity, which has limited sensitivity and accuracy (Liu et al., 2022). Fluorescence (FL) detection has higher sensitivity and photothermal sensors possess lower background than colorimetry; therefore, the multimodal (colorimetric, FL, and photothermal) method can improve reliability via mutual confirmation, which can also satisfy different testing requirements according to sensitivity and available instruments/m. Therefore, an efficient strategy is to rationally design a self-cascade system and develop a multimode sensing platform by combining colorimetric signals with other diverse modalities assisted by the physicochemical properties of nanozymes (Liang & Yan, 2019).

Metal–organic frameworks (MOFs) are promising biomimetic enzyme candidates. Such materials provide excellent host platforms to accommodate diverse functional nanomaterials, thereby improving the enzyme activity, developing the cascade catalytic processes, and achieving multimode sensing (Huang et al., 2021). Among various functional guest materials, possessing low toxicity, easy synthesis, and excellent luminescence, carbon dots (CDs) have been successfully used to produce luminescence and improve the catalytic performance of composites (Liu et al., 2020; Zhao et al., 2022). Moreover, Au NPs have been extensively used for biomedical applications, and individual hybrid materials based on AuNPs and MOFs as peroxidase mimics have been constructed for cascade reactions and glucose sensing (Mu et al., 2023; Tong et al., 2023). However, multiguest assembly, multichannel glucose, and maltose sensing in cascade systems are yet to be developed.

Herein, we successfully constructed an efficient self-cascade nanozyme biosensor comprising N-rich CDs and Au NPs co-loaded on MIL-53(Fe)-NH2 via a facile microwave reaction (Scheme 1 ) and first achieved multimodal detection of glucose and maltose. The loading of CDs enhanced the peroxidase-like activity of the composite, exhibiting an extremely high affinity for H2O2 (K m : 0.011 mM). During sequential oxidation, blue ox-TMB quenched the FL of the composite and increased the solution temperature under 808 nm laser irradiation because of the photothermal effect. Based on the excellent activity and changes in colorimetric/FL/photothermal signals, three sensitive channels were developed to detect glucose and maltose with low limits of detection (LODs) of 12/7.5/1.1 and 18/11.3/1.4 μM, respectively. Furthermore, real food sample detection was performed and positive results were achieved, revealing the excellent accuracy and value of this method.

Citric acid was obtained from Tianjin GuangFu Technology Development Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China). Sodium acetate (NaAc) was obtained from Beijing Chemical Works Co. Ltd. (Beijing, China). Ethylenediamine was obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). 2-Aminoterephthalic acid, TMB, uric acid, fructose, sucrose, maltose, and glucose were all provided by Aladdin (Shanghai, China). N,N-Dimethylformamide (DMF) and acetic acid were purchased from Tianjin Jingdongtianzheng Precision Chemical Reagent Factory (Tianjin, China). Iron chloride hexahydrate and ascorbic acid were supplied by Tianjin FuChen Reagent Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China). Milk was purchased from a school supermarket.

Powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) was performed on a Rigaku D/Max-2550 diffractometer. Scanning and transmission electron microscopy were performed on a JEOL JSM-6700F SEM and an FEI Tecnai G2 S-Twin F20 transmission electron microscope, respectively. Adsorption spectra were collected on a Shimadzu UV-2550 spectrophotometer. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra were measured on a Thermo ESCALAB 250 spectrometer with monochromatized Al Kα excitation. Photoluminescence was determined using a HORIBA Scientific Fluoromax-4 spectrofluorometer. A JES-FA 200 EPR spectrometer was used to collect the EPR spectra.

Density functional theory (DFT) calculations were performed in the CP2K code. All calculations employed mixed Gaussian and planewave basis sets. Core electrons were represented with norm-conserving Goedecker–Teter–Hutter pseudopotentials, and the valence electron wavefunction was expanded in a double-zeta basis set with polarization functions along with an auxiliary planewave basis set with an energy cutoff of 450 eV. The Perdew–Burke–Ernzerhof (PBE) generalized gradient approximation exchange-correlation functional was used. Each configuration was optimized with the Broyden–Fletcher–Goldfarb–Shanno (BFGS) algorithm with an SCF convergence criterion of 1.0 × 10−5 au. The van der Waals correction of Grimme's DFT-D3 model was also adopted. The Gibbs free reaction energy (ΔG) is calculated using the binding strengths between the catalyst and the POD-like intermediates
(1)
∆ G = ∆ E + ∆
E ZPE
− T ∆ S ,
where ∆ E is the DFT-calculated adsorption energy of the intermediate. The zero-point energy (ZPE) contribution is given by
(2)
E ZPE
=
∑ i
hv i
2
,
where h and
v i
are Planck's constant and vibrational frequency, respectively, which are calculated based on the localized harmonic oscillator approximation with a displacement of 0.01 Å. During the frequency calculations, only the mobile adsorbates and the atoms at the active site were considered while all other atoms on the catalyst were treated as fixed. The vibrational entropy contribution ( S ) is calculated using
(3)
S =
k B
∑ i
h
v i
k B
T
e
h
v i
k B
T
− 1
− ln
1 −
e
− h
v i
k B
T
.

MIL-53(Fe)-NH2 and CDs@MIL-53(Fe)-NH2 were prepared according to our previous report with some modification (Liu et al., 2024). Briefly, 1.350 g of FeCl3·6H2O, 0.524 g of 2-aminoterephthalic acid (BDC-NH2), and 1 mL of acetic acid were dissolved in 15 mL of DMF. Afterward, different amounts of CD powder (4, 9, and 12 mg) were added to the above solution and the mixture was subjected to a microwave at 170 °C for 60 min. The resulting solution was centrifuged for 4 min at 8000 ×g, and the solid was then washed three times with ethanol and DMF before vacuum drying. The final products were denoted as CDs@MIL-1 to CDs@MIL-3. The same procedure was used to create the control product, pure MIL-53(Fe)-NH2, but without CDs.

Au NPs were obtained by reduction of NaBH4 to HAuCl4 (Mu et al., 2023). Au/CDs@MIL-53(Fe)-NH2 was prepared by mixing the obtained Au NP solution (2, 3, 4, 5, and 6 mL) and CDs@MIL-2 (30 mg) dispersed in 10 mL of water at room temperature for 8 h. The solid products were denoted as Au/CDs@MIL-1 to Au/CDs@MIL-5, respectively. The control product (Au@MIL-53(Fe)-NH2) was prepared by the same process, but replacing CDs@MIL-2 with MIL-53(Fe)-NH2, denoted as Au/MIL. All the solid products were obtained by centrifuging, washing with water, and dried at 75 °C.

A total of 50 μL of 1 mg/mL materials, 40 μL of 10 mM TMB, and 20 μL of 10 mM H2O2 were added to 1890 μL of acetate buffer solution (pH 3.5). Afterward, UV–vis spectra were measured. Steady-state kinetics was evaluated by monitoring the absorbance at 652 nm by changing the concentrations of H2O2 and TMB. Kinetic parameters were calculated using the Michaelis–Menten equation (eq. 4)
(4)
1 V
=
K m
V max
1
S
+
1
V max

A total of 50 μL of 2 mg/mL Au/CDs@MIL-3 and 100 μL of 0.2 M glucose/maltose were added in 50 μL of carbonate buffer (pH 10) and incubated at 35 °C for 20 min. Then, 60 μL of 20 mM TMB and 1740 μL of acetate buffer (pH 3.5) were introduced and reacted at 35 °C for 20 min. Finally, absorbance was measured at 652 nm. The same method was used for Au NPs, CDs@MIL-2, and Au NPs + CDs@MIL-2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Multimodal detection of glucose and maltose
ce:section.ce:para: For colorimetric and FL sensing, a series of different concentrations of glucose and maltose were added into the above reaction system. Absorbance at 652 nm and FL intensity at 450 nm under 360 nm excitation were monitored by UV–vis and FL spectroscopy, respectively. For photothermal sensing, the reaction mixture was irradiated by a 808 nm laser with a power density of 2 W/cm2, and the corresponding temperatures of the different sensing systems with varied glucose and maltose concentrations were obtained by a thermal imaging camera.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Immobilization of CDs/Au NPs in MIL-53(Fe)-NH2
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the design of the self-cascade nanozyme, two functional guests, namely, CDs and Au NPs, were synthesized and encapsulated in MIL-53(Fe)-NH2 (Scheme 1). The prepared CDs were brownish-black, with a uniform size of approximately 3.5 nm, and a broad diffraction peak was observed in the XRD patterns (Fig. S1A–E). The high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image of CDs further showed the typical well-ordered lattice fringes with a spacing of 0.21 nm, corresponding to the (100) facet of graphene (Fig. S1F) (Li et al., 2022; Ru et al., 2021; Zhang et al., 2024). The prepared Au NPs were purple, with a uniform size of approximately 2.3 nm, and the distance between two neighboring planes was measured to be 0.24 nm in HRTEM, corresponding to the (111) planes of cubic Au (Fig. S2) (Chen et al., 2016). Compared with MIL-53(Fe)-NH2, CDs@MIL retained its long spindle-like morphology; however, its size decreased as the amount of loaded CDs increased (Fig. S3). The morphology and size of the composite were almost unchanged after further loading Au NPs (Fig. S4).

In the following characterizations, the optimized CDs@MIL-2 and Au/CDs@MIL-3 were used as representatives for this study because of their best peroxidase- and oxidase-like activities. In Fig. 1A–D, dark spherical particles of CDs and Au NPs with lattice spacing values of 0.21 and 0.24 nm could be observed clearly, respectively. The uniformly distributed Au NPs with small size on CDs@MIL-2 were observed in the dark-field TEM images (Fig. 1E, F). Energy dispersive X-ray mapping indicated the existence of Fe, Au, C, N, and O in Au/CDs@MIL-3 (Fig. 1G). The content of Au was highest at 0.77 wt% in Au/CDs@MIL-3, as determined by ICP analysis (Table S1). In these composites, the main diffraction peaks of MIL-53(Fe)-NH2 at 2θ of 9.2° (002), 12.5° (101), 16.8° (103), 18.6° (200), 25.3° (220), and 28.0° (211) were confirmed by the XRD patterns (Fig. 1H, S5A), which showed good crystallinity (Wu et al., 2021; Zheng et al., 2018). N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms showed that MIL-53(Fe)-NH2 exhibited a typical I-type curve with a BET surface area of 509.13 m2/g (Fig. S5B). XPS spectra proved the rich nitrogen functional groups in CDs (Fig. S6). As shown in Fig. S7, Au 4f was fitted to two peaks at 84.3 and 88 eV owing to the presence of Au0 in Au NPs (Mu et al., 2023). The Fe 2p spectra showed two notable peaks at 725.3 and 711.4 eV, which could be ascertained to be Fe 2p1/2 and Fe 2p3/2, respectively, coupled with a satellite peak at 715.2 eV, thereby indicating the presence of Fe3+ species in the FeO cluster (Cheng et al., 2022). Notably, after loading CDs and Au NPs, the binding energy of the Fe 2p3/2 peak is minimally shifted, implying the alteration of the chemical environment and density change of the electron cloud of Fe3+ in the composites, which is caused by interactions in CDs or Au NPs (Fig. 1I). The minimal change in the baseline of the Fe 2p spectra may be caused by coating CDs and Au NPs on the partial surface of MIL-53(Fe)-NH2. Owing to the optical properties of BDC-NH2 ligands and CDs, all MOF-based nanozymes exhibited blue emission with an optimal emission and excitation wavelength of 450 and 360 nm, respectively (Fig. S8) (Yang et al., 2024; Zhao et al., 2023). The FL intensity of MIL-53(Fe)-NH2 minimally increased and then decreased after the incorporation of CDs and Au NPs (Fig. 1J). These results revealed the successful construction of the CDs and Au NPs co-hybridized MOF nanozyme.

Peroxidase-mimetic activity was investigated using H2O2 as the oxidant and TMB as the substrate (Fig. 2A) (Chang et al., 2024). Notably, MIL-53(Fe)-NH2 exhibited peroxidase-like activity, whereas pure CDs could not catalyze the oxidation of H2O2. Interestingly, the MIL-53(Fe)-NH2 activity was enhanced after loading CDs, and the CD@MIL-2 composite exhibited the best catalytic performance. In addition to TMB, CDs@MIL-2 showed good catalytic activity for oxidation of other typical substrates, such as o-phenylenediamine and 2,2′-azinobis-(3-ethylbenzthiazoline-6-sulfonate) (Fig. 2B). Even at a low concentration of H2O2, such an enzyme exhibited excellent activity at 45 °C, indicating high affinity for H2O2 (Fig. S9). After loading Au NPs, the activity of the Au/CD@MIL composite was minimally decreased and Au/CDs@MIL-3 exhibited the best performance (Fig. 2C). The optimal pH was 3.5, and the optimal temperature of 60 °C was higher than that of CDs@MIL-2 (Fig. S10). Au/MIL without CDs showed worse peroxidase-like performance compared with Au/CDs@MIL-3, suggesting the prominent effect of CDs on catalytic activity (Fig. S11).

Steady-state kinetic parameters are crucial for quantitatively evaluating the properties of nanozymes (Wu et al., 2024). Michaelis–Menten kinetics were observed for the reaction rates (Fig. 2D–I), and Lineweaver–Burk plots (Fig. 2D–I inset) were obtained from the Michaelis–Menten curves. The calculated K m of CDs@MIL-2 was lower than that of MIL-53(Fe)-NH2, indicating a higher affinity for H2O2 and TMB in CDs@MIL-2 (Wang et al., 2024). In particular, the K m value for H2O2 of CDs@MIL-2 (0.011 mM) was the lowest among the known MOF-based nanozymes to the best of our knowledge, which was 336 times lower than that of the natural HRP (Table S2) (Gao et al., 2007). These results revealed that CDs@MIL-2 showed great potential for improving the LOD of H2O2 and constructing self-cascade catalytic systems connected with H2O2. After loading Au NPs, the K m for H2O2 increased to some degree; however, it still showed exceptional affinity compared with the other peroxidase mimics.

The catalytic activity of CDs@MIL-2 was suppressed without O2 because nanozymes normally use O2 to catalyze the oxidation of substrates (Fig. S12A). The type of active oxygen species was further determined by adding different scavengers into the reaction solution, including isopropanol, sodium azide (NaN3), and p-benzoquinone, revealing that ·OH was the active oxygen species in the catalytic process (Fig. S12B) (Yang et al., 2021). ·OH was further identified by EPR with DMPO as the spin probe, and the distinctive signals of 1:2:2:1 related to ·OH were observed (Fig. S12C). The stronger signal intensity of CDs@MIL-2 demonstrated that CDs did enhance the peroxidase-like activity of MIL-53(Fe)-NH2, which was attributed to the faster electron transfer rates (Fig. S12D) (Wang et al., 2021).

Moreover, DFT calculations were performed to elucidate the catalytic mechanism and the advantages of CDs in the catalytic performance (Fig. S12E, F and Fig. 2J, K). In the catalytic reaction, H2O2 first adsorbed at the Fe sites of MIL-53(Fe)-NH2 and CDs@MIL-2, with adsorption energies of −0.59 and − 0.86 eV, respectively. Subsequently, the reaction *H2O2 → *O + H2O was a sequential exothermic process, releasing heats of −1.72 and − 1.44 eV, and the addition of TMB resulted in adsorption energies of −0.43 and − 0.81 eV, respectively. Further, *O + *TMB → *O + *ox-TMB was the rate-determining step, which absorbed energies of 1.53 and 0.74 eV, respectively. Finally, ox-TMB was released from the system while a second TMB was added to the system, which ultimately generated H2O and ox-TMB, completing the POD-like catalytic process (Zhu et al., 2024). Compared with MIL-53(Fe)-NH2, the energy barrier of the rate-controlling step of CDs@MIL-2 was significantly reduced. Meanwhile, the adsorption strengths of H2O2 and TMB by CDs@MIL-2 were greatly larger than that of MIL-53(Fe)-NH2. The calculation results provided powerful evidence for the enhancing effect of CDs on the POD-like activity of MIL-53(Fe)-NH2.

The feasibility of establishing self-cascade oxidation within Au/CDs@MIL-3 was investigated using glucose and maltose as substrates. A series of comparative experiments showed that the performance of cascade oxidation based on the Au/CD@MIL-3 composite was better than the simple mixing of CDs@MIL-2 and Au NPs as well as Au/MIL, proving the host–guest synergistic effect of CDs, Au NPs, and MIL-53(Fe)-NH2 in the constructed nanozyme (Fig. 3A) (Chai et al., 2023). In addition, a similar phenomenon could be observed when replacing glucose with maltose (Fig. 3B). Absorption decreased when adding catalase (CAT) into Au/CDs@MIL-3 with glucose or maltose, indicating the generation of H2O2 during the reaction process (Fig. 3C). EPR spectra detected the ·OH species originating from the decomposition of H2O2 by peroxidase-like Au/CDs@MIL-3(Fig. 3D). Fig. 3E shows that maltose could not be decomposed by Au/CDs@MIL-3 during the detection process. The oxidation products of glucose and maltose were gluconic acid and maltose acid (Fig. 3F), respectively, based on a specific colorimetric assay through derivative experiments (Zhao et al., 2016). The self-cascade sequential oxidation process based on the Au/CD@MIL-3 nanozyme was achieved by the synergistic effect of Au NPs and CDs@MIL-2 (Fig. 3G). First, Au NPs attached on CDs@MIL-2 with oxidase-like activity oxidized glucose and maltose to gluconic acid and maltose acid, respectively, accompanied by the formation of H2O2. Then, H2O2 was further decomposed into ·OH by CDs@MIL-2 with excellent peroxidase-like activity. Finally, the colorless substrate TMB was oxidized to blue ox-TMB by ·OH.

On the basis of the obvious color change observed in the self-cascade reaction of Au/CDs@MIL-3, a simple colorimetric method was developed for sensing glucose and maltose. The sensing conditions were optimized as follows: 50 μg/mL Au/CDs@MIL-3 and a series of different concentrations of glucose/maltose were added into carbonate buffer (pH = 10) and incubated at 35 °C for 20 min. Thereafter, TMB and acetate buffer (pH = 3.5) were added and incubated at 35 °C for 20 min (Fig. S13). The absorbance at 652 nm increased as glucose or maltose concentrations increased (Fig. 4A). The change in absorbance (ΔA = A-A0, where A and A0 are the absorbance at 652 nm before and after glucose/maltose addition, respectively) showed good linearity to glucose/maltose concentration in the range of 0–1 mM, and the LODs at an S/N ratio of 3 for glucose and maltose were 12 and 18 μM, respectively (Fig. 4B, C).

Interestingly, ox-TMB could quench the FL of Au/CDs@MIL-3 (Fig. S14). The quenching mechanism was subsequently explored. A considerable overlap existed between the excitation spectrum of Au/CDs@MIL-3 and the absorption spectrum of ox-TMB. The FL lifetime of Au/CDs@MIL-3 barely changed after the reaction, and no new UV–vis absorption peak was observed. These results revealed that the quenching phenomenon was caused via the inner filter effect (Zu et al., 2017). Based on the effective quenching behavior, an FL assay was developed for sensing glucose and maltose. The FL at 453 nm decreased with an increase of glucose or maltose concentrations (Fig. 4D). The intensity of (FF0)/F0 (where F and F0 are the FL intensity at 453 nm before and after glucose/maltose addition, respectively) showed good linearity to glucose/maltose concentration in the range of 0–2/0–1 mM, and the LODs for glucose and maltose were 7.5 and 11.3 μM, respectively (Fig. 4E, F).

Moreover, ox-TMB is an alternative photothermal agent with a superior photothermal effect and can convert the excitation energy of a near-infrared laser into heat, thereby increasing the temperature (Fig. S15) (Luo et al., 2023). Therefore, an additional photothermal temperature sensing for glucose and maltose was workable. The temperature of the solution increased as glucose or maltose concentrations increased (Fig. 4G). The change in temperature (ΔT = T-T0, where T and T0 are the temperature before and after glucose/maltose addition, respectively) showed good linearity to glucose/maltose concentration in the range of 0–2/0–1.5 mM, and the LODs for glucose and maltose were 1.1 and 1.4 μM, respectively (Fig. 4H, I).

Thus, from the above discussion, the designed Au/CD@MIL-3 nanozyme could achieve the colorimetric, FL, and photothermal temperature detection for both of glucose and maltose. Notably, majority of the previous reported methods for glucose detection rely on natural enzymes and expensive equipment such as HPLC. The reported enzyme-free cascade platforms are shown in Table S3, and almost all realized single colorimetric sensing, which commonly limits the sensitivity from tens to thousands of micromoles and is easily interfered by diverse factors. Compared with these, by integrating colorimetry with fluorometry and temperature signal, the simple and low-cost three-channel sensing of Au/CDs@MIL-3 showed favorable reliability owing to the built-in cross reference correction by each other. More importantly, the sensing sensitivity of such a nanozyme gradually enhanced from 12 to 1.1 μM, which is second only to AuNPs@PCN-224(Fe) in the enzyme-free system (Tong et al., 2023). As for maltose sensing, most of the reported methods are based on chromatography, which is severely limited by expensive testing cost (Hassanzadeh et al., 2022). To our knowledge, a simple, low-cost, nanozyme-based optical assay is first developed herein to detect maltose. Therefore, our study integrated the advantages of previous reports such as enzyme-free self-cascade systems and developed a novel three-in-one nanozyme (Dong et al., 2015; Tong et al., 2023). In terms of performance, Au/CDs@MIL-3 exhibited excellent blue FL, extremely high affinity for H2O2, and unique triple detection for glucose/maltose. The triple detection method integrated several advantages such as colorimetric visualization, high FL sensitivity, and low photothermal background, which showed considerable reliability and excellent sensitivity in all related nanozyme-mediated cascade sensing platforms.

The selectivity study was performed by introducing other substances (e.g., fructose, sucrose, uric acid, and ascorbic acid) into the sensing systems. In Fig. 4J–L, all proposed sensing (colorimetric, FL, and photothermal temperature) assays showed good specific recognition ability. Notably, sucrose is a more common ingredient in foods and can affect the physical properties of food such as rheology and the molecular interaction between the nanozyme and substrate (Yang et al., 2018; Yang et al., 2020). In addition, sucrose can be hydrolyzed to glucose and fructose under several processing conditions. Therefore, the anti-interference ability was studied by adding sucrose into the system. Fig. S16 reflects good anti-interference ability owing to the similar intensity of all groups. In fact, the results of our method will be affected if sucrose can be consistently hydrolyzed during the production and storage of food.

Standard addition tests were then conducted to investigate the practicability of Au/CDs@MIL-3 as a self-cascade catalytic nanozyme in a milk sample. In addition, complex food matrices, such as proteins, carbohydrates, lipids, minerals, and phytochemicals, affect assay accuracy (Mao et al., 2021). Therefore, to remove matrix compounds, the milk sample was first pretreated by diluting and sonicating for 30 min and then adding 3 % acetic acid solution and incubating for 20 min. The mixture was centrifuged and filtered through a membrane of 0.22 μm. From the standard curve, the concentrations of the target analytes in the food samples were obtained. The good recovery rates of 99.12 %–101.46 %, 99.09 %–101.47 %, and 98.20 %–101.80 % were calculated for the colorimetric, FL, and temperature assays, respectively, and the relative standard deviations (RSDs) were low and less than 2 % (Table 1 ). These results indicated the reliability of the Au/CD@MIL-3 nanozyme in food analysis.

Herein, we constructed an efficient enzyme-free self-cascade platform based on an MOF–nanozyme system by integrating two functional guests, namely, Au NPs and CDs, into MIL-53(Fe)-NH2 and first realized the detection of glucose and maltose using a multisignal response. The incorporation of N-rich CDs endowed MIL-53(Fe)-NH2 with high peroxidase-like activity because of the increased adsorption of H2O2 and TMB and the reduced energy barrier of the rate-controlling step, which could effectively oxidize TMB to a blue ox-TMB in the presence of H2O2. The introduction of Au NPs conferred the composite with oxidase-like activity that could oxidize glucose and maltose, along with the generation of H2O2. The cascade reaction was performed because of the oxidase-peroxidase-like activity and the “three-in-one” effects from the product ox-TMB, namely, color change, FL quenching, and temperature increase. Thus, the developed colorimetric/FL/photothermal detection system for glucose and maltose exhibited excellent sensitivity, reliability, and practicability. This study provides new insights into the design of multimodal self-cascade sensing platforms in MOF–nanozyme systems using host–guest synergistic effects for food safety and human health.

Junxue Liu: Writing – original draft, Validation, Investigation, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Yufei Wang: Formal analysis, Data curation. Mingqin He: Resources. Xiaohong Chen: Resources. Chenyu Zhou: Data curation. Jiyang Li: Validation, Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We thank the Jilin Province Science and Technology Development Plan (20230101026JC), the ‘111 Center’ (B17020) and the Specific Research Fund of the Innovation Platform for Academicians of Hainan Province, China (YSPTZX202321) for financial support.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2024.142485.
